rationale: A0 shows netsh.exe running at High integrity under a standard user account (host-01\user-01) to add an inbound TCP firewall rule on port 450 with the name '[x] testing', spawned from a bare cmd.exe with no visible parent command context. EV-001 confirms the rule was written to the Windows Firewall registry key, and EV-003/EV-004 confirm the rule was deleted ~5 seconds later — the transient add-then-delete pattern is consistent with a connectivity test or a cleanup step in a script, but is also a known technique used to probe firewall state or briefly open a port. The '[x] testing' name and non-standard port 450 are mildly anomalous; however, no inbound connection, payload delivery, or lateral movement is evidenced, and the rule was fully reverted (EV-004). The absence of any network connection events or further suspicious child processes caps confidence in a malicious verdict.
